questions.questionText: With reference to Swadeshi Movement consider the following statements: 1. It contributed to the revival of the indigenous artisan crafts and industries. 2. The National Council of Education was established as a part of the Swadeshi Movement. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is correct. **The Swadeshi Movement included boycotts and public burning of foreign goods and**contributed to the revival of the indigenous artisan crafts and industries. The Swadeshi Movement celebrated our **indigenous fabrics, weavers and the country’s artisans. August 7 has been chosen as National Handloom Day as it **was the day that the Swadeshi Movement was started in 1905**. Self-reliance or ‘atma shakti’ was encouraged. The swadeshi spirit also found expression in the establishment of swadeshi textile mills, soap and match factories, tanneries, banks, insurance companies, shops, etc.**

Statement 2 is correct. The National Council of Education-Bengal (or NCE-Bengal) was an organisation founded **by Indian nationalists in Bengal in 1906 **as part of the Swadeshi Movement. It was set up to organise a system of education— literary, scientific and technical—on national lines and under national control.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In the context of Indian National movement, consider the following:
I. More Indian representation in Legislative councils
II. Holding of Civil Services examinations in India
III. Separation of the judiciary from the executive
IV. Allocation of greater public funds for irrigation
V. Reduction of land revenue and cut in military expenditure

Which of the above demands were put forward by the leaders of Indian National Congress towards the British colonial government in the late nineteenth century and the initial years of the twentieth century?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian National movement, consider the following: I. More Indian representation in Legislative councils II. Holding of Civil Services examinations in India III. Separation of the judiciary from the executive IV. Allocation of greater public funds for irrigation V. Reduction of land revenue and cut in military expenditure Which of the above demands were put forward by the leaders of Indian National Congress towards the British colonial government in the late nineteenth century and the initial years of the twentieth century?

options.a: I, II, IV and V only
options.d: I, II, III, IV and V

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. During the early years (**1885-19**05) the Indian National Congress was under the influence of Moderate leaders, most of whom were English educated Indians. The moderate leaders were influenced by Western political ideas and practices, especially by the political philosophy of liberalism. The Congress programme during the early phase (**1885-19**05) can be divided under three categories: 1) constitutional and administrative reforms 2) social reforms and defence of civil rights and 3) economic reforms.** Option I is correct**. One of the major demands of the** moderate leaders was proper representation of Indians on the **Legislative Councils as well as increase in the power of these Councils**. This demand flowed from their belief that Indians must have a formal voice in law-making and administration.** Option II is correct**. The Moderates strongly opposed the practice of holding the Civil Services examination only in London. They demanded that the examination be**held in India as well**, so that capable Indians could enter higher administrative services. This demand was closely linked to the broader goal of**Indianisation of administration**and resistance to racial discrimination.** Option III is correct**. One of the important constitutional demands of the Moderates was the**separation of judicial **functions from executive authority**. They believed that justice could not be impartial if executive officials also exercised judicial powers. This demand reflected their commitment to rule of law and civil liberties.** Option IV is correct**. The Moderates raised several economic issues affecting the masses. They argued that British rule had led to**poverty, famines, and agricultural distress**. As part of their economic programme, they demanded**greater public expenditure on irrigation**, which they believed would reduce dependence on monsoons and prevent famines** Option V is correct**. The Moderate leaders criticised the**high land revenue demand**and the**excessive military **expenditure**of the colonial government. They demanded reduction in revenue burden on peasants and diversion of funds from the army to welfare and development activities. This demand was a core part of their critique of colonial economic policy.

questions.questionText: In the context of national movement in India in the later half of the 19th century, consider the following pairs: Organisation Founded by I. Landholder’s Society Raja Ram Mohan Roy II. Indian National Association Surendranath Banerjee III. Bombay Association Jagannath Shanker Seth How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. Long before the Indian National Congress came into existence, educated Indians had begun to organise themselves politically through regional associations. These bodies reflected early political consciousness and provided platforms to articulate grievances against British rule. However, they were limited in reach and often represented specific social or regional interests.**Pair I is incorrect**.**The Zamindari Association**, also known as the**Landholders’ Society**, was established in**1838**to protect the interests of landlords in Bengal. The association was formed by**Dwarkanath Tagore and other influential **Bengal zamindars**.**Pair II is correct**. The**Indian National Association**, often known as**Indian Association**, was founded in**1876**by**Surendranath Banerjee and Anand Mohan Bose**. The main purpose of this organisation was to**promote political **awareness among Indians**,**create a strong public opinion against unjust British policies**, and**unite Indians under a **common political programme**. It also aimed at the**political, intellectual, and material advancement of the people**and later sponsored an**All-India Conference in 1883**.**Pair III is correct**. The** Bombay Association**was the first political organization in the Bombay Presidency founded by**Jagannath Shanker Seth in 1852** to vent public concerns against the British.

options.b: A theory that argued that the Congress was formed as a safe outlet to the hostile attitude of the British towards the
options.c: A theory which believed that the British and the ex-rebels of 1857 should amicably resolve their differences against
options.d: An idea aimed at promoting goodwill among the Indian peopl

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.** A.O Hume himself had said that his aim was to provide, to use his own expression, a ‘safety valve’ providing control to the “great and growing forces generated by” the British themselves. This has been juxtaposed with W.C. Bonnerjee’s statement that Hume was acting under the direct advice of Dufferin. These two facts studied together gave rise to the argument that the Indian National Congress grew out of the British conspiracy, the aim of which was to provide a peaceful and constitutional outlet to the discontent amongst the educated Indians and thus provide against the threat to the Raj.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Indian National movement, who among the following proposed the “Lightning Conductor
Theory” in the context of the formation of the Indian National Congress?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian National movement, who among the following proposed the “Lightning Conductor Theory” in the context of the formation of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The**Lightning Conductor Theory **was** propounded by Gopal Krishna Gokhale to explain the circumstances **surrounding **the** formation of **the**Indian National Congress (INC) in 1885**. - It posits that politically conscious Indian leaders wanted to create a national platform to express their political and economic demands. However, they knew that if Indians directly founded such an organization, British officials would strongly oppose and suppress it. - Therefore, they**strategically used A.O. Hume**(a retired British civil servant)**as a "lightning conductor"**(a protective shield or catalyst)**to safely establish and unite nationalist forces under the INC**, even if it appeared under the guise of a British-initiated "**safety valve." **Thus, unlike the**Safety Valve Theory** (which presents Congress as a British strategy), the Lightning Conductor Theory emphasizes Indian initiative and political foresight, with Hume acting as a shield rather than the real architect of the movement.

statementLines: In the context of the freedom struggle of India, which of the following statements with reference to the First
Session of the Indian National Congress are correct?
I. It was held at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in Bombay.
II. Lord Dufferin was the Viceroy of India at the time.
III. The session was presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee.
IV. In this session, the resolution on foreign policy was adopted which condemned the annexation of Upper Burma by the British.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: In the context of the freedom struggle of India, which of the following statements with reference to the First Session of the Indian National Congress are correct? I. It was held at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in Bombay. II. Lord Dufferin was the Viceroy of India at the time. III. The session was presided over by Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee. IV. In this session, the resolution on foreign policy was adopted which condemned the annexation of Upper Burma by the British. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The formation of the Indian National Congress in 1885 marked a decisive turning point in the history of the Indian national movement. It provided, for the first time, a common political platform where educated Indians from different regions could come together to articulate shared grievances.**

Statement I is correct**. The first session of the Indian National Congress was**held**in**Bombay (now Mumbai)**in**December 1885**, at the**Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College**.**

Statement II is correct**. At the time of the first session of the Indian National Congress,**Lord Dufferin**was the Viceroy of India. He held the office from**1884 to 1888**.**

Statement III is correct**. The first session of the Indian National Congress was**presided over by Womesh Chandra **Bonnerjee**.**

Statement IV is correct**. The first session adopted a total of 9 resolutions. Few of the resolutions were: - In one resolution they demanded the appointment of a royal commission for enquiring into Indian affairs on which Indians would be adequately represented.  Another resolution demanded abolition of the Indian Council of the Secretary of State for India. The Congress wanted the Secretary of State to be responsible directly to the British Parliament. This demand was based on the idea that the British people were just and fair, if properly informed they would never deviate from the right path.  There**was a resolution on foreign policy which condemned the annexation of Upper Burma.**  Other resolutions covered subjects such as functions of Central and Provincial Legislative Councils, holding of simultaneous examinations for the civil services in Britain and India and the need to reduce expenditure on army etc.

questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian National Congress, consider the following information: INC Session (Year) President Important decision / event I. 1896 Badruddin Tyabji Vande Mataram was sung at this session of the Congress by Rabindranath Tagore. II. 1906 Dadabhai Naoroji It was announced that Swaraj was the goal of India’s political efforts. III. 1907 Rash Behari Ghosh The Congress witnessed a split between the Moderates and the Extremists In how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Information in row I is incorrect:**The**1896 Calcutta session**of the Indian National Congress holds historical importance because it was**during this session**that**Rabindranath Tagore sang Vande Mataram for the first time,**giving the song a national political resonance. However, the**President of the 1896 session was Rahimtullah M. Sayani, not Badruddin Tyabji.**Badruddin Tyabji**had earlier**presided over the 1887 Madras session.**Information in row II is correct:**The**1906 Calcutta session, presided over by Dadabhai Naoroji,**marked a watershed moment in the ideological evolution of the Indian National Congress.**For the first time, the Congress explicitly declared Swaraj (self-government) as its political objective. **At this stage, Swaraj implied self-government within the British Empire, not**Poorna Swaraj**or complete independence (which was adopted much later in**1929 at the Lahore Session**). Information in row III is correct: **The**1907 Surat Session of the INC**was**presided over by Rash Behari Ghosh.**This session is historically significant for the**split between the Moderates and the Extremists**, arising from sharp differences over political methods, leadership, and attitude towards British rule.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer **The**Poona Sarvajanik Sabha**was an important early political organisation that emerged in western India during the nineteenth century. It represented one of the earliest attempts by educated Indians to engage with the colonial administration in a peaceful and organised manner. Options II, III and IV are correct: In 1870, Poona Sarvajanik Sabha was formed by M.G.Ranade, S.H. Chiplonkar-and **others in the Bombay Presidency. Other leaders like SH Sathe, GV Joshi also played an important role in the **foundation of the Sabha. ** Option I is incorrect: Vasudev Balwant Phadke was not primarily associated with the foundation of the Sabha.**He was a prominent social reformer and public intellectual of western India. Vasudev Balwant Phadke was associated with**revolutionary activities**and armed resistance against British rule, not with the formation of this constitutional political organisation.** Option V is incorrect: Pandurang Mahadev Bhapat was born in 1880 so would not be associated with the foundation **of Sabha. **He was one of the freedom fighters who is known for uniquely combining the diverse revolutionary and Gandhian ideologies.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In the context of Indian Freedom struggle, consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Moderate leaders of the Indian National Congress were not satisfied with certain provisions of the
Indian Councils Act, 1892.
Statement II: The Indian Councils Act, 1892 introduced a system of direct election for the members of the Central
Legislative assembly but only for a limited number of seats.

Statement III: As per the provisions of the Indian Council Act, 1892 the member of the Central Legislative council can only discuss the annual Financial Statement presented by the government but had no voting rights over it.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian Freedom struggle, consider the following statements: Statement I: The Moderate leaders of the Indian National Congress were not satisfied with certain provisions of the Indian Councils Act, 1892. Statement II: The Indian Councils Act, 1892 introduced a system of direct election for the members of the Central Legislative assembly but only for a limited number of seats. Statement III: As per the provisions of the Indian Council Act, 1892 the member of the Central Legislative council can only discuss the annual Financial Statement presented by the government but had no voting rights over it. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**Indian Councils Act, 1892**was an act of the British Parliament that aimed to make limited reforms in the legislative councils of British India. It was introduced during the**tenure of Lord Lansdowne**as the Viceroy of British India and marked a modest step toward Indian representation in governance, but it**fell far short of the expectations **of the Indian National Congress. **

Statement I is correct:**The**Moderate leaders of the Indian National Congress**(INC) were advocating for gradual reforms and greater Indian participation in governance through constitutional means.**They were dissatisfied with the Indian Councils Act of 1892 because the Act failed to bring about any substantial **expansion of Indian participation in governance**. The reforms were largely cosmetic and did not satisfy the Moderates’ demand for greater representation and effective legislative powers.**

Statement II is incorrect:**The Indian Councils Act of 1892 introduced the principle of representation in the council. The**act avoided the term ‘election’**and introduced an element of indirect election for selection of non-officials in the Central Legislative Council. The Indian Councils Act of 1892 did not introduce direct elections in the true sense. Instead, it provided for the**nomination of certain non-official members**: - At the**Centre**, non-official members of the Central Legislative Council were nominated by the**Viceroy**, based on recommendations from the**provincial legislative councils**and the**Bengal Chamber of Commerce**. - In the**Provinces**, non-official members were nominated by the**Governors**on the recommendations of bodies such as**district boards, municipalities, universities, trade associations, zamindars, and chambers of commerce**. Thus, the Act made only a**limited and indirect provision for elections**in filling some non-official seats at both Central and provincial levels. Significantly, the term**“election” was not used in the Act. **This**Act was criticised at the 1892 and 1893 sessions of the INC mainly because the principle of direct-election had **not been introduced. **

Statement III is correct and explains Statement I:**As per the provisions of the Indian Councils Act 1892, **the members of Central Legislative Council were allowed to discuss the annual Financial Statement presented by the Government. However,**the Financial Statement was presented as an unalterable document (with no right to vote **for members of the Central Legislative Council).**Members could only present their observations which could have influence on the budget in subsequent years, not on the budget of the year under consideration. Thus,**Members could not vote on the budget**, nor were they allowed to move amendments to it. As a result, the councils remained largely advisory bodies, lacking effective control over the executive. This procedural limitation was**another major reason**for the**Moderates’ unhappiness**, as it made the councils largely**symbolic rather than functional**.

statementLines: In 1920, which of the following changed its name to "Swarajya Sabha"?
questions.questionText: In 1920, which of the following changed its name to "Swarajya Sabha"?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **When Gandhiji and his followers gained control of the All-India Home Rule League,**they changed its name to 'Swaraj **Sabha' (Hindi for Home Rule League).** At the same time, they also changed its constitution to allow it to serve as a vehicle for the Non-cooperation movement. This enabled Gandhi to combine cadres of the Home Rule League and Muslim league for the Non-cooperation movement.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In the context of modern Indian history, a paper titled “The Wants and Means of India” was written by:
questions.questionText: In the context of modern Indian history, a paper titled “The Wants and Means of India” was written by:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **“The Wants and Means of India” was one of Dadabhai Naoroji’s most significant contributions. **In this work, first presented as a paper in 1870 at London’s Society of Arts, Dadabhai Naoroji systematically analysed India’s economic condition under British rule. He asked whether India was producing enough to meet its own needs and answered this by quantifying and explaining India’s extreme poverty and by calculating India’s per capita income using land revenue and production data.  In “The Wants and Means of India,” Dadabhai Naoroji pioneered economic comparisons between India and other countries, particularly the United States and the United Kingdom, in the belief that statistics on Indian poverty would astound and shock British citizens and that well-crafted comparisons would make them viscerally uncomfortable. He introduced statistics into politics in this work, making it one of his most valuable contributions.

statementLines: With reference to Anushilan samiti formed during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following
statements:
I. The Calcutta Anushilan Samiti was formed by Sachindra Nath Sanyal in 1902.
II. The Samiti got its name from one of Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay’s plays.
III. Bande Matram, an English newspaper, popularised the ideas of the Samiti to the masses.
IV. Some of its members were arrested and tried in the Muzaffarpur Conspiracy case (1908).
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Anushilan samiti formed during the freedom struggle of India, consider the following statements: I. The Calcutta Anushilan Samiti was formed by Sachindra Nath Sanyal in 1902. II. The Samiti got its name from one of Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay’s plays. III. Bande Matram, an English newspaper, popularised the ideas of the Samiti to the masses. IV. Some of its members were arrested and tried in the Muzaffarpur Conspiracy case (1908). Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer **Anushilan Samiti was one of the secret revolutionary organisations operating in Bengal in the first quarter of the 20th century.**

Statement I is incorrect: **The Anushilan Samiti was formed in Calcutta by Satish Chandra Basu/Bose and Barrister Pramathanath Mitra in 1902 after encouragement from Sister Nivedita and Swami Saradananda. At the same time Jnendranath Bose and Hemchandra Kanungo established a secret society in Midnapur town. It was directly associated with and supervised by the Calcutta Anushilan Samiti.**Sachindra Nath Sanyal in 1913 established a branch of the organization called the Anushilan Samiti in Patna. **

Statement II is correct: The Anushilan Samiti got its name from one of Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay’s plays **titled Anushilan-tatva (Theory of Discipline). **This suitably encapsulates the functioning and goal of the Samiti as well, which was a wide umbrella of enhancing physical, mental, moral and spiritual faculties, with the ultimate goal of presenting a strong resistance to the foreign rule.**

Statement III is correct: The Bande Mataram **was an English language weekly newspaper published from Calcutta founded in 1905 by Bipin Chandra Pal and edited by Sri Aurobindo.**Its aim was to prepare Indians to struggle for **complete independence. **Similarly to newspaper Yugantar, which popularised Anushilan Samit ideas in Bengali,**while Bande Mataram did the **same in English. **

Statement IV is correct: **Khudiram Bose and Prafulla Chaki threw a bomb on the carriage of the Chief Presidency Magistrate Douglas Kingsford of Calcutta in Muzaffarpur district in present-day Bihar. While Khudiram Bose was arrested Prafulla Chaki committed suicide. Khudiram Bose was tried along with his accomplices, Mrityunjay Chakraborty and Kishorimohan Bandopadhyay.** The trial of this incident, known as the Muzaffarpur Conspiracy **Case (1908). They were members of Anushilan Samiti. Thus some of the members of the Anushilan Samiti were **arrested and tried in the Muzaffarpur conspiracy case. **

questions.questionText: With reference to Revolutionary activities during the early 20th century in India, consider the following pairs: Cases Related To Associated Personality I. Delhi-Lahore Conspiracy Case, 1912 Assasination Attempt on Governor General Lord Hardinge Aurobindo Ghosh II. Alipore Conspiracy Case, 1908 Assasination of Sir William Hutt Curzon Willie Vasudev Balwant Padhke III. Nashik Conspiracy Case, 1910 Assasination of District Collector of Nashik A.M.T Jackson Anant Lakshman Kanhere In how many of the rows given above is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Row I is incorrect: The Delhi-Lahore Conspiracy Case (1912) is related to the arrest and trail of Rash Behari Bose **and Sachindranath Sanyal who carried out a the bomb attack on Viceroy Lord Hardinge during his ceremonial **procession through Chandni Chowk in Delhi. **Although Hardinge survived, the subsequent investigation led to the arrest and trial of several revolutionaries, resulting in the conviction and execution of Basant Kumar Biswas, Amir Chand, and Avadh Behari for their involvement in the conspiracy.**Aurobindo Ghosh is related to the Alipore Conspiracy case. **Row II is incorrect: The Alipore Conspiracy Case(**1908-19**09) (also known as the Manicktolla Bomb Case or **Muraripukur Conspiracy) involved the arrest of the entire Anushilan Samiti(not Abhinav Bharat)**, including Aurobindo Ghosh and Barindra Kumar Ghosh, for charges of conspiracy and waging war against the British Crown. While Aurobindo Ghosh was acquitted, Barindra Ghosh and Ullaskar Dutt, who was involved in bomb-making, were initially sentenced to death, later commuted to life imprisonment. The trial also witnessed the killing of Narendra Gosain, a government approver, by fellow revolutionaries Satyendranath Bose and Kanailal Dutt inside jail. Curzon Willie assasination case of 1909 is related to July 1, 1909, when one of the members of Abhinav Bharat, Madan Lal Dhingra shot and killed Curzon Wyllie in the hall of the Imperial Institute.**Row III is correct: The Nashik Conspiracy Case (1909) was related to the assassination of A.M.T. Jackson, the **British Collector of Nashik, by Anant Lakshman Kanhere, a member of Abhinav Bharat**. It was found that the killing was part of a conspiracy to overthrow the British government in India by means of armed revolution.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Keshav Chandra Sen is associated with the**establishment of Tabernacle of New Dispensation and Indian Reform **Association.**He was a social reformer and leader of the Brahmo Samaj, a socio-religious movement that advocated for monotheism and social reforms. - He**founded the Tabernacle of New Dispensation**, also known as Naba Bidhan, in 1868 as a new religious institution that combined elements of Hinduism, Christianity, Islam, and Buddhism. - He also**founded the Indian Reform Association in 1870**as a secular organization that aimed to promote education, women’s rights, legal reforms, and social welfare**Keshav Chandra Sen was not associated with the establishment of the Calcutta Unitarian Committee**, which was founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Dwarkanath Tagore, and William Adam in 1823 as a platform to propagate Unitarianism and rationalism.

It was established by Khan Abdul Gaffar Khan in Punjab. II. The journal BharatMata is associated with the society. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**

Statement I is incorrect: **In Punjab, revolutionary activities were a result of famines, rising land revenue and other taxes, practices of begar and because of the events in Bengal.**Ajit Singh (and not Khan Abdul Gaffar Khan) founded a **secret society named Anjuman-i-Mohisban-i-Watan in Lahore **to spread revolutionary ideas among the youth. Before Ajit Singh’s group turned to extremism, it was active in urging non-payment of revenue and water rates among Chenab colonists and Bari Doab peasants. Other leaders included Aga Haidar, Syed Haider Raza, Bhai Parmanand and the radical Urdu poet, Lalchand ‘Falak’.**

Statement II is correct: The journal BharatMata is associated with Anjuman-i-Mohisban-i-Watan. **Lala Lajpat Rai and Lala Hansraj brought out the Punjabee (with the motto of self-help at any cost).

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**Indian revolutionaries took their activities abroad in need for shelter, for the possibility of bringing out revolutionary literature that would be immune from the Press Acts and for the quest for arms.**Pair I is correct: India Home Rule Society**or ‘India House’**was started in London in 1905 by Shyamji Krishnavarma **as a centre for Indian students, a scholarship scheme to bring radical youth from India. This society**brought out a **journal**by the name of**‘The Indian Sociologist’. **Savarkar and Lala Hardayal became the members of India House.**Pair II is correct:**In 1914, Virendranath Chattopadhyaya with the assistance of several **revolutionaries in Paris and Switzerland, and Abinash Bhattacharya met Baron Oppenheim of Germany in September 1914. This resulted in the formation of the Indian Friendship Society of Germany, which later became** known as the Indian Independence **Committee (IIC) or the Berlin Committee in 1914.**Pair III is incorrect:**The**Commonwealth of India League was not founded by Bhikaji Cama. **V. K. Krishna Menon had joined Annie Besant’s Home Rule for India League to fight for Indian independence, inspired by the Irish Home Rule movement. The organisation was later renamed the Commonwealth of India League. At this point, Besant resigned and left the League under Menon’s leadership.

questions.questionText: With respect to Modern Indian History, consider the following statements about the Komgata Maru incident: I. The Ghadar Party headquartered in San Francisco was formed as a reaction to this incident. II. The Shore Committee is related to this incident. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**In 1914, the Komagata Maru incident unfolded as the Japanese steamship sailed from Hong Kong to Vancouver, carrying passengers from British India. Most of the passengers were denied entry, leading to the ship’s forced return to India. 

Statement I is incorrect: **The**Ghadr Party headquarterd at San Francisco **and branches along the US coast**was **formed in 1913**, whereas the**Komagata Maru incident occurred in 1914 at Vancouver**. Hence, chronologically, the party could not have been established in reaction to this incident.**

Statement II is correct: **The**Shore Committee was established to advocate for the human rights of the passengers **in the Komagata Maru Incident. ** The shore committee was composed of local South Asian descent activists who raised funds to hire lawyer J. Edward Bird to defend the passengers’ rights of entry. The court allowed only one case to be heard and based on that case summarily denied all passengers entry, except 20 passengers who could prove their prior residency in Canada.

statementLines: With reference to legislative developments during the second half of the nineteenth century under British rule in
India, consider the following pairs:
Act / Bill Important Provision I.
The Indian Arms Act, 1878 It would be a criminal offence if any Indian, Britisher or Anglo-Indian carries arms without license
II. Ilbert Bill, 1883 Proposed to allow senior Indian magistrates to preside over criminal cases involving

questions.questionText: With reference to legislative developments during the second half of the nineteenth century under British rule in India, consider the following pairs: Act / Bill Important Provision I. The Indian Arms Act, 1878 It would be a criminal offence if any Indian, Britisher or Anglo-Indian carries arms without license II. Ilbert Bill, 1883 Proposed to allow senior Indian magistrates to preside over criminal cases involving British or European subjects in India. III. Vernacular Press Act, 1878 The Indian publisher of a newspaper in any language to enter into a bond not to publish anything which is likely to excite the feelings of disaffection to the government. How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. In the late nineteenth century, the British colonial state introduced a series of laws to strengthen administrative control and suppress political dissent. These measures were often justified in the name of law and order, but in practice they widened racial discrimination and curtailed civil liberties.**Pair I is incorrect**.**The Indian Arms Act of 1878**was enacted with the objective of**disarming Indians while **exempting Europeans from its restrictions**. The**Act was discriminatory as it did not apply to the British, **Europeans, Anglo Indian and certain government personnel**. It required Indians to obtain licences for possessing arms, whereas Europeans were generally excluded from such requirements.**Pair II is correct**.**The Ilbert Bill of 1883**sought to remove racial discrimination in the judicial system by**allowing **senior Indian magistrates to preside over criminal cases involving British or European subjects in India.**Until then, Europeans could only be tried by European judges. The proposal triggered strong opposition from the European community in India, revealing the deep racial prejudices embedded in colonial governance.**Pair III is incorrect**.**The Vernacular Press Act of 1878**, was specifically designed to**curb the freedom of the Indian
- **language press**, which had begun to criticise British policies. It empowered the government to demand security deposits from publishers and to**seize printing presses if objectionable material was published**.**English-language newspapers were deliberately kept outside its scope**.

statementLines: With reference to Delhi Durbar (1911) during the British rule in India, consider the following statements:
I. It was organised to mark the succession of King George V of Britain.
II. During the Delhi Durbar of 1911, the partition of Bengal was annulled.
III. The shifting of the imperial capital of British ruled India from Calcutta to Delhi was announced at the Durbar.
IV. Jana Gana Mana composed by Rabindranath Tagore was first sung in Delhi Durbar of 1911.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Delhi Durbar (1911) during the British rule in India, consider the following statements: I. It was organised to mark the succession of King George V of Britain. II. During the Delhi Durbar of 1911, the partition of Bengal was annulled. III. The shifting of the imperial capital of British ruled India from Calcutta to Delhi was announced at the Durbar. IV. Jana Gana Mana composed by Rabindranath Tagore was first sung in Delhi Durbar of 1911. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**The**Delhi Durbars were grant events organised by the Viceroys to mark the coronation of Emperors**or Empresses. Hence, these were also known as Coronation Durbars. Durbar which means a ‘court of a ruler’ in Persian was adopted by the British from the Mughals. The durbars were primarily held to legitimize and popularise colonial rule. Statements I and III are correct: **The**Durbar of 1911 marked the succession of King George V. **This Durbar was historic for two reasons: one it was the only Durbar attended by the Emperor himself and second, this was where the**shifting of the Imperial Capital from Calcutta to Delhi was announced.**

Statement II is correct:**The**annulment of the Bengal partition was announced at the Delhi Durbar of 1911 **by King George V mainly to curb the menace of revolutionary terrorism. The annulment came as a rude shock to the Muslim political elite. Bihar and Orissa were taken out of Bengal and Assam was made a separate province.**

Statement IV is incorrect:** Jana Gana Mana was first sung at the Calcutta session of the Indian National Congress in 1911, not at the Delhi Durbar. The performance of “Jana Gana Mana” took place at Bharat Sabha, a single-storied structure located on Bow bazar Street in Kolkata. While some saw it as a welcome song for the imperial majesties at the DelhiDurbar, Tagore clarified its intent as praising the Dispenser of India’s destiny.

In 1915, during an unsuccessful revolutionary attempt, Jatin Mukerjea popularly known as Bagha Jatin gave his life fighting a battle with the police at Balasore.**Rash Bihari Bose, **Raja Mahendra Pratap, Lala Hardayal, Abdul Rahim, Maulana Obaidullah Sindhi, Champak Raman Pillai, Sardar Singh Rana, and Madam Cama were some of the prominent Indians Ghadar Leaders who carried on revolutionary activities and propaganda outside India.**Options 1 and 2 are incorrect: **Barindra Kumar Ghosh and Jogesh Chandra Chatterjee were not associated with the Ghadar party.  Barindra Kumar Ghosh published the Bengali weekly Jugantar. statementLines: Two separate Home Rule Leagues were established by Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak with the objective of attaining self-government for India. In this context, consider the following regions:
I. Karnataka
II. Bombay
III. Central Provinces

questions.questionText: Two separate Home Rule Leagues were established by Annie Besant and Bal Gangadhar Tilak with the objective of attaining self-government for India. In this context, consider the following regions: I. Karnataka II. Bombay III. Central Provinces IV. Madras V. Berar The Tilak’s Home Rule League operated in which of the above regions?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Bal Gangadhar Tilak**had established the Home Rule League in**April 1916**at the** Bombay Provincial Congress**which was held in what is now known as**Belagavi district, Karnataka**. The term “Home Rule League” was inspired from a political party and movement of similar nomenclature in Ireland for the objective of attaining self-government. Although Annie Beasant had also launched a Home Rule League, the two Leagues neither merged, nor did any situation of friction arise between them as they had demarcated their respective areas of activity. Thus, whereas** Tilak operated in Maharashtra**(excluding Bombay city),**Karnataka**,** Berar **and the** Central Provinces**; Beasant worked in the rest of India, with South as her primary focus. Tilak promoted his League by touring Maharashtra and delivering lectures to popularize and promulgate the essence of Home Rule League. Thus,**only **Options I, III and V are correct.**

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Examine these two statements carefully and
select the correct answer using the code given below.
Statement I: Dadabhai Naoroji argued that what was being drained out was ‘potential surplus’ that could generate more economic development in India if invested in India.
Statement II: Imperialists believed that India was brought into the large capitalist world market and that was in itself a progress towards modernisation.
Code:
questions.questionText: Examine these two statements carefully and select the correct answer using the code given below. Statement I: Dadabhai Naoroji argued that what was being drained out was ‘potential surplus’ that could generate more economic development in India if invested in India. Statement II: Imperialists believed that India was brought into the large capitalist world market and that was in itself a progress towards modernisation. Code:

options.a: Both the statements are individually true and statement II is the correct explanation of statement I.
options.b: Both the statements are individually true, but statement II is not the correct explanation of statement I.
options.c: Statement I is true, but statement II is false.
options.d: Statement I is false, but statement II is tru

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct: **Dadabhai Naoroji did argue that what was being drained out of India by the British was the ‘potential surplus’ that could generate more economic development in India if invested in India. He proposed the ‘drain of wealth’ theory in 1867, in which he estimated the net national profit of India and the effect of colonial rule on it. He identified certain factors that caused external drain and criticized the British for exploiting India’s resources, markets, and manpower.**

Statement II is correct:**Imperialists did believe that India was brought into the large capitalist world market and that was in itself a progress towards modernization. They justified their colonial rule by claiming that they were civilizing and modernizing India, which they considered backward and stagnant. They also argued that India benefited from the free trade and infrastructure development under British rule.**However, statement II is not the correct explanation of statement I,** because Naoroji’s argument was not based on the imperialist view of India’s integration into the world market, but on his own calculation of India’s national income and expenditure. He did not accept the imperialist claim that India was progressing under British rule, but rather exposed the adverse effects of British exploitation on India’s economy and society.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Home Rule League Movement in India witnessed a decline after 1918.
Statement II: In 1918, Bal Gangadhar Tilak was deported to Mandalay on the charge of sedition.
Statement III: There was difference of opinions between the Moderates and Extremists section of the Home Rule of
League.

questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: The Home Rule League Movement in India witnessed a decline after 1918. Statement II: In 1918, Bal Gangadhar Tilak was deported to Mandalay on the charge of sedition. Statement III: There was difference of opinions between the Moderates and Extremists section of the Home Rule of League. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I.
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I.
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer **

Statement I is correct: **The Home Rule agitation gradually fizzled out after 1918. By 1919, it had completely obliterated.**

Statement II is incorrect. **The British authorities arrested Bal Gangadhar Tilak in 1908 for writing an allegedly seditious article in ‘Kesari’. He was deported to Mandalay. However, this event occurred much before the Home Rule Movement (1916–18) and therefore cannot be the reason for its decline after 1918.**Tilak’s decision to go to England at **the end of 1918 to pursue a libel case **he had filed against Valentine Chirol, made the movement leaderless.**

Statement III is correct. **The Moderates who had joined the movement after Annie Besant’s arrest were pacified by talk of Montagu’s reforms and Besant’s release. On the other hand,**Extremists wanted to launch passive resistance **against the British**. This kept the Moderates away from activities of the league from September 1918 onwards. The announcement of Montagu-Chelmsford reforms, further divided the nationalist ranks. Thus,**the difference of opinion **is clearly seen between extremists and moderates here. **Statement III explains Statement I: The difference of opinion between the Moderates and extremists lead to **decline of Home Rule League Movement. **

statementLines: In the context of colonial India, the Brati Samiti, the Suhrid Samiti and the Sadhena Samiti were primarily known for:
questions.questionText: In the context of colonial India, the Brati Samiti, the Suhrid Samiti and the Sadhena Samiti were primarily known for:

options.a: Establishing provisional Indian governments in Singapore and Kabul.
options.b: Organising peasant movements against the oppressive conduct of European indigo planters.
options.c: Promoting the use of domestic goods and boycott of foreign goods during the Swadeshi Movement.
options.d: Propagating the ideas of Theosophical society in Eastern India.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**The partition of Bengal in 1905 infuriated people all over India. The struggle that occurred against this partition was known as the Swadeshi Movement in Bengal. During the Swadeshi Movement, a large number of national volunteer bodies or samitis sprang up**for aiding the **cause of national education, and for spreading the messages of boycott and swadeshi. **Some of the important samitis among them were the Dawn Society, the Anti-Circular Society, the Swadesh Bandhab Samiti,** the Brati Samiti, the Suhrid Samiti and the Sadhena Samiti. ** These samitis preached the essentials of swadeshi and boycott, took up social work during famines and epidemics, imparted physical and moral training, organised crafts and national schools and set up arbitration committees and village societies.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Kadambini Ganguly one of the first female graduate in India, in the Indian National Congress Session of 1890 gave a lecture in English - becoming the first woman to do so at the INC. Who among the following presided over this session of the Indian National Congress?
questions.questionText: Kadambini Ganguly one of the first female graduate in India, in the Indian National Congress Session of 1890 gave a lecture in English - becoming the first woman to do so at the INC. Who among the following presided over this session of the Indian National Congress?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer **Kadambini Ganguly took admission to Bethune College and graduated in 1882 from Calcutta University. She and Chandramukhi Basu became two of the first female graduates in the entire British Empire when they received their degrees in 1883. It was their success that made Bethune College introduce FA (First Arts) and graduation courses in 1883. In 1884, she became the first woman to get admitted to the Calcutta Medical College. Kandambini Ganguly’s social work to empower India also heaped praise from a number of national leaders of the time. In the 1889 session of the Indian National Congress which had six women, Kadambini being one of them. She moved the vote of thanks at the session and was hailed by Annie Besant as being a “symbol that India’s freedom would uplift India’s womanhood”. In the next session of the INC in 1890 in Calcutta, Kadambini gave a lecture in English-becoming the first woman to do so at the INC.**Pherozshah Mehta presided over the Congress session held in Calcutta (1890) and was twice elected the President **of the Reception Committee when the Congress sessions met in Bombay (1889 and 1904). Kadambini Ganguly, the **first woman graduate of Calcutta University, addressed the Congress session of 1890 held at Calcutta. **

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Indian Freedom Struggle, why was October 16, 1905 observed as a day of mourning all over Bengal?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian Freedom Struggle, why was October 16, 1905 observed as a day of mourning all over Bengal?

options.a: The decision to partition Bengal was officially announced on this day.
options.b: The partition of Bengal formally came into effect on this day.
options.c: The Indian Newspapers (Incitement to Offences) Act was enacted to suppress the Swadeshi Movement.
options.d: The day was observed to commemorate the victims of the Bengal famine of 1770.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The Partition of Bengal in 1905 marked a decisive shift in the nature of the Indian national movement. It transformed political discontent into**mass-based public protest**, especially in Bengal, which eventually strengthened Indian Freedom Struggle.**October 16, 1905 was the day on which the Partition of Bengal was formally implemented**by the colonial government. The partition was widely perceived as a**deliberate political move to weaken nationalism by dividing **people**, particularly on communal lines i.e. Dividing Hindu and Muslims to break the rising discontent against Britishers and Indian unity. In response, the people of Bengal observed the day as a**day of mourning**, marked by fasting, processions, and symbolic gestures of unity. The observance reflected**public grief and protest against the **enforced division**, not merely opposition in words.**

Option a is incorrect**. The British government**announced the decision to partition Bengal in July 1905**, several months before its implementation in October 1905. While the announcement itself caused widespread resentment,**October 16 did not mark the announcement of the Partition of Bengal**.**

Option c is incorrect**. The**Indian Newspapers (Incitement to Offences) Act**was enacted in**1908**, much after the events of 1905. It was aimed at suppressing nationalist and Swadeshi writings in the press.**

Option d is incorrect**. October 16 1905 has no relation to the Bengal famines of 1770.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Which of the following statements about the Swadeshi Movement in Bengal in 1905 are correct?
1. It was marked by the mass mobilisation through the organisation of ‘Samitis’.
2. It was marked by the mass mobilisation through labour strikes.
3. It advocated a constructive programme of self-help or ‘atmashakti’.
4. In the sphere of education, national schools were established.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements about the Swadeshi Movement in Bengal in 1905 are correct? 1. It was marked by the mass mobilisation through the organisation of ‘Samitis’. 2. It was marked by the mass mobilisation through labour strikes. 3. It advocated a constructive programme of self-help or ‘atmashakti’. 4. In the sphere of education, national schools were established. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is correct: **The Swadeshi Movement was a mass movement that was marked by the**organization of **samitis**, or associations. These samitis were responsible for organizing protests, boycotts, and other forms of civil disobedience.**

Statement 2 is correct: **The Swadeshi Movement witnessed**widespread labor strikes **in various industries and sectors. Workers actively participated in the movement, demanding better working conditions and higher wages.**

Statement 3 is correct: **The Swadeshi Movement emphasized the**concept of ‘atmashakti,’ **which means self-help or self-reliance. It aimed to promote indigenous industries, encourage economic self-sufficiency, and reduce dependency on imported British goods.**

Statement 4 is correct: **In the sphere of education,**national schools were established**. These schools were designed to provide a nationalist education that would help to promote Indian self-rule.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to history of Modern India, consider the following statements regarding the differences between the Moderates and the Extremists within the Indian National Congress during the early 20th century:
I. The Moderates believed in widening the democratic base of freedom struggle by attracting masses on large scale while Extremists believed in individual heroic action.
II. Unlike the Moderates, the Extremists were primarily influenced by western liberal ideas such as liberalism, democracy, and equity.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to history of Modern India, consider the following statements regarding the differences between the Moderates and the Extremists within the Indian National Congress during the early 20th century: I. The Moderates believed in widening the democratic base of freedom struggle by attracting masses on large scale while Extremists believed in individual heroic action. II. Unlike the Moderates, the Extremists were primarily influenced by western liberal ideas such as liberalism, democracy, and equity. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect. **The**Extremists had immense faith in the capacity of masses**to participate and to make sacrifices. The Extremists’ emphasis on mass participation and on the need to broaden the social base of the movement was a progressive improvement upon Moderate politics.**The early nationalists (Moderates) did a great deal to awaken the national sentiment, even though they could not **draw the masses to them. **The Moderates did not believe in masses. They believed that the movement should be limited to middle class intelligentsia; and masses were not yet ready for participation in political work.**

Statement II is incorrect. The moderate leaders were influenced by western liberal thought and European history, **on the other hand, the**Extremists were inspired by Indian history**, its glorious cultural heritage.
Moderates like Dadabhai Naoroji, Surendranath Banerji, Pherozeshah Mehta, Gopal Krishna Gouale, M. G. Ranade, were uncritical admirers of Western political values. They held the concept of equality before law, freedom of speech and press and the principle of representative government as inherently superior to their traditional Hindu polity which they defined as 'Asiatic despotism'.

questions.questionText: Which of the following was/were the primary reasons for the 'Surat Split' in the Indian National Congress in 1907? I. The domination of leaders belonging to the socialist and communist ideologies within the Congress. II. The opposition by the Moderate leaders to the election of Rashbehari Ghosh as the President of the Indian National Congress. III. The opposition by the Moderate leaders within the Congress to the Extremist’s proposal to take the Swadeshi Movement outside Bengal. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect. A left wing emerged in the Indian National Congress during the 1920s and 1930s. **Ideas of Marx and Socialist thinkers inspired many groups to come into existence as socialists and communists. These ideas resulted in the rise of a left wing within the Congress, represented by Jawaharlal Nehru and Subhash Chandra Bose. These young nationalists were inspired by the Soviet Revolution. They began advocating radical solutions for economic, political and social ills of the country.**

Statement II is incorrect. The Extremists wanted**the 1907 session to be held in Nagpur**with BG Tilak or Lala Lajpat **Rai as the President**. They also wanted a reiteration of the swadeshi, boycott and national education resolutions.**The **Moderates**wanted the session to be held at Surat in order to exclude Tilak from the presidency, since a leader from the host province could not be session president. Instead, they**wanted Rashbehari Ghosh as the presiden**t and sought to drop the resolutions on swadeshi, boycott and national education. The**extremists opposed the election of **Rash Behari Ghosh as president**. Both sides adopted rigid positions, ultimately leading to split. 

Statement III is correct. The Extremists wanted to extend the Boycott and Swadeshi Movement to regions outside **Bengal**. They also wanted to include all forms of associations (such as government service, law courts, legislative councils, etc.) within the boycott programme and thus start a nationwide mass movement. The**Moderates were not **in favour of extending the movement beyond Bengal.**

statementLines: Which of the following were the joint demands raised by the Indian National Congress and the All-India Muslim
League under the Lucknow Pact of 1916?
I. To grant Dominion Status to India within the British Commonwealth.
II. The tenure of the legislative councils should be fixed at five years.
III. At least half of the members of the Provincial Governors’ Executive Councils should be Indians.
IV. There should be elected majorities in the representative assemblies at both Centre and provincial levels.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following were the joint demands raised by the Indian National Congress and the All-India Muslim League under the Lucknow Pact of 1916? I. To grant Dominion Status to India within the British Commonwealth. II. The tenure of the legislative councils should be fixed at five years. III. At least half of the members of the Provincial Governors’ Executive Councils should be Indians. IV. There should be elected majorities in the representative assemblies at both Centre and provincial levels. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**Lucknow Pact of 1916**was a**historic political agreement between the Indian National Congress and the All
- **India Muslim League**, concluded during their**annual sessions held at Lucknow in December 1916. **It marked a rare phase of Congress–League unity, wherein**both organisations presented joint constitutional **demands to the British Government**. It marked the high point of Hindu–Muslim unity during the national movement.**

Statement I is incorrect:**The**Lucknow Pact (1916) demanded**that the British Government should declare that it would confer**self-government on Indians**at an early date,**not Dominion Status**. The formal and unambiguous**demand for Dominion Status emerged later**, particularly in the**Nehru Report (1928)**and was politically asserted by the Congress only in the late 1920s.**

Statement II is correct: **The Pact proposed that** both Central and Provincial Legislative Councils should have a fixed **tenure of five years. **This provision was aimed at ensuring stability and continuity in legislative functioning, bringing Indian legislatures closer to established parliamentary practices.**

Statement III is correct:**The Pact sought greater Indian participation in executive governance. It clearly stated that a**minimum of 50% of the members of both the Viceroy’s and Provincial Governors’ Executive **Councils should be Indians. **This was aimed at Indianisation of administration at both the Central and Provincial levels.**

Statement IV is correct: The demand under the pact was **the representative assemblies at the central as well as provincial level should be further expanded with an elected majority and more powers given to them Knowledge Base:**Presidents of the 1916 Lucknow Sessions**- Indian National Congress:**Ambika Charan Mazumdar**- All-India Muslim League:**Muhammad Ali Jinnah**

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Indian national leaders expressed dissatisfaction with the Montagu’s Statement, also known as the
August Declaration of 1917.
Statement II: The Montagu’s Statement vested the British Government with the sole authority to determine the pace and extent of India’s constitutional advancement.
Statement III: The Montagu’s Statement made no provision for universal adult franchise in elections to legislative bodies.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: The Indian national leaders expressed dissatisfaction with the Montagu’s Statement, also known as the August Declaration of 1917. Statement II: The Montagu’s Statement vested the British Government with the sole authority to determine the pace and extent of India’s constitutional advancement. Statement III: The Montagu’s Statement made no provision for universal adult franchise in elections to legislative bodies. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I.
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I.
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**

Statement I is correct:**The**Montagu Statement (August Declaration**),**issued on 20 August 1917 by Secretary of **State for India, Edwin Samuel Montagu**in the British House of Commons, formally declared that**British policy **is**aimed at increasing Indian participation in every branch of administration**and the**gradual development of self
- **governing institutions**, with the ultimate objective of responsible government in India, as an integral part of theBritish Empire.**Despite this apparent concession, Indian political leaders expressed dissatisfaction**, as the declaration failed to meet their concrete political expectations.**

Statement II is correct:**Although the Declaration spoke of increasing Indian participation in administration and the gradual development of self-governing institutions, it made it clear that the**British Government alone would **determine the nature, pace, and timing of constitutional advance**.**Indian leaders strongly resented this unilateral approach**, as it implied that the British would judge what was suitable for Indians, without Indian consent.**This was one of the two principal objections raised by nationalists. **The**other objection**was that**no specific time frame was provided for the attainment of responsible government**, making the promise vague and uncertain.**

Statement III is correct: **The**Montagu Statement made no provision for universal adult franchise in elections to **legislative bodies**, which was inconsistent with nationalist demands. However,**this omission was not among the **principal objections raised against the Declaration**; the core dissatisfaction lay in the absence of a definite timeline and the continued dominance of British discretion over constitutional progress. As can be seen in the demands of Lucknow pact there was no demand for universal adult franchise rather it talked about as broad a franchise as possible. It was in the Nehru Report 1928 when the leaders started formally demanding Universal Adult franchise. Thus absence of Universal adult franchise was not the reason for dissatisfaction amongst the Indian Leaders against the declaration.**Conclusion **Both Statements II and III are correct**, but**only Statement II explains**the dissatisfaction expressed by Indian **political leaders. **Therefore, the**correct answer is: b) Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains **Statement I.**

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. ** Option I is correct. **In June 1917, Annie Besant and her associates, B.P. Wadia and George Arundale, were arrested. This invited nationwide protests**. **In a dramatic gesture, Sir S. Subramaniya Aiyar renounced his knighthood. Those who had stayed away earlier like**Madan Mohan Malviya**, Surendra Nath Banerjea and M.A. Jinnah now enlisted as members of the Home Rule Leagues to record their solidarity with the internees and their condemnation of the Government’s action.** Option II is correct. **The Home Rule agitation was later joined by Motilal Nehru, Jawaharlal Nehru, Bhulabhai Desai, Chittaranjan Das, K.M. Munshi, B. Chakravarti, Saifuddin Kitchlew, Madan Mohan Malaviya, Mohammad Ali Jinnah, Tej Bahadur Sapru and Lala Lajpat Rai. Some of these leaders became heads of local branches of Annie Besant’s League.**Mohammad Ali Jinnah led the Bombay division. ** Option III is correct. George Arundale**was the organising secretary of the Annie Besant’s League.** Option IV is correct.**Bipin Chandra Pal from**1908-11**, stayed in England where he developed a new political thought which he called the Empire-idea. He pleaded for reconstitution of the British empire as a federal union in which India, Great Britain and all the British selfgoverning colonies would co-operate as equal and free partners. On coming back to India he started a monthly journal, the Hindu Review (1913), and tried to popularize this idea.**He then joined the Home Rule Movement of Annie **Besant and Bal GangadharTilak. ** Option V is incorrect: Gopal Krishna Gokhale** died on 19 February 1915. Thus, he was not part of the home rule movement.

questions.questionText: With reference to the Defence of India (Criminal Law Amendment) Act, 1915, consider the following statements: I. It had a sunset clause and it was to expire six months after the end of the First World war. II. Its provisions became the basis for the controversial Rowlatt Act or the Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act of 1919. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**The Defence of India (Criminal Law Amendment) Act, 1915 was enacted as an emergency criminal law during the First World War. Its primary objective was to curtail nationalist and revolutionary activities in India that were perceived as a threat to British rule by granting extraordinary powers to the colonial government.**As per Section 4 of the Act, its provisions were to expire six months after the end of the First World war. **

Statement II is correct: After the end of the First World War**,**several extraordinary powers granted under the **Defence of India Act, 1915**, such as detention without trial and special judicial procedures, were sought to be continued by the colonial government.**These provisions directly formed the basis for the enactment of the Rowlatt **Act, 1919** (Anarchical and Revolutionary Crimes Act, 1919).

Statement I is correct:**Within the Indian National Congress, the Moderates backed the British Empire during the war out of a sense of duty, while the Extremists, including Bal Gangadhar Tilak after his release in June 1914, also extended support to the war effort.**At the beginning of the First World War, Indian political opinion supported the British. statementLines: With reference to the sessions of the Indian National Congress (INC) during the Indian National Movement,
consider the following events:

questions.questionText: With reference to the sessions of the Indian National Congress (INC) during the Indian National Movement, consider the following events: I. The year of INC session which was for the first time presided over by a foreigner. II. The year of INC session which was for the first time presided over by a woman. III. The year of INC session which was for the first time presided over by a Muslim. Which one of the following is the correct chronological order of the above events?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The annual sessions of the Indian National Congress act as**milestones in the political history of modern India**. They reflect how a small organisation of educated Indians gradually transformed into a**broad-based national movement**. Each phase of this journey is marked by changes in leadership, social representation, and political alignment.**Option I**. The early phase of the Indian National Congress was marked by the presence of**British liberals who were **sympathetic to Indian political aspirations**. In this context, the Congress elected**George Yule**, a British businessman and political moderate, as its president in**1888**. George Yule presided over the**Allahabad session**, making it the**first occasion when a foreigner chaired a Congress **session**. This was historically significant because it reflected the**initial strategy of constitutional politics**, where Indian leaders believed that cooperation with liberal elements in Britain could help in securing reforms.**Option II**. The entry of women into the leadership of the national movement was a**gradual but decisive **transformation**in Indian politics. In**1917**,**Annie Besant**became the president of the Indian National Congress. This event symbolised not only**women’s participation**, but also the growing role of**Home Rule agitation**within the Congress framework.**Option III**. The Indian National Congress also reflected the**religious diversity of Indian society**in its leadership choices. In**1887**,**Badruddin Tyabji**, a distinguished lawyer and reformist, presided over a Congress session. He was the**first Muslim president of the Congress**, and his election conveyed a strong message of**composite nationalism**. Hence the correct chronological order of the INC Sessions starting from the earliest is:**INC Session **Year ** III. The first INC session, presided over by a Muslim.
1887 I. The first INC session, presided over by a non-Indian. 1888 II. The first INC session, presided over by a woman. 1917

statementLines: With reference to British Indian administration, the Welby Commission was appointed to examine which of the following aspects of colonial governance?
questions.questionText: With reference to British Indian administration, the Welby Commission was appointed to examine which of the following aspects of colonial governance?

options.a: The working of provincial administration under the Indian Councils Act 1909.
options.b: The financial relations between the central and provincial governments in India.
options.c: The conditions of Indentured Indian labour employed in British-owned plantations.
options.d: The distribution of expenses of administration between Britain and the Indian government.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. During the late nineteenth century, Indian nationalists increasingly questioned the colonial financial system. A major concern was the continuous outflow of Indian revenues to Britain. In response to growing criticism, the British government appointed commissions to review specific aspects of colonial administration. The**Welby Commission**, formally known as the**Royal Commission on Indian Expenditure (1895)**, was appointed to examine the**expenditure of Indian revenues in Britain**, commonly referred to as**Home Charges**. These included payments for pensions of British officials, interest on public debt raised in Britain, and administrative expenses incurred in England. Indian nationalists criticised these expenses as excessive and a drain on India’s resources. The commission acknowledged the issue but largely defended British expenditure practices.**

Option a is incorrect**. The**Welby Commission**was not concerned with legislative or administrative functioning of provinces. Issues related to**provincial administration, legislative councils, and representation**were examined under**constitutional reform measures**, particularly the**Indian Councils Act, 1892**, and later by the**Morley–Minto Reforms **(1909)**.**

Option b is incorrect**. The issue of**financial relations between the central government and provincial governments**became important in the early twentieth century. It was examined by bodies such as the**Meston Committee (1920)**and later by the**Simon Commission (1927)**, and was finally addressed comprehensively in the**Government of India **Act, 1935**. The Welby Commission did not deal with fiscal federalism within India.**

Option c is incorrect**. The**conditions of Indian labour**, especially in plantations and overseas employment, were examined by**Indentured Labour Committees**, such as the**Sanderson Committee (1910)**and later by inquiries leading to the**abolition of the indenture system in 1920**. These bodies dealt with labour welfare and migration issues. The Welby Commission had**no connection with labour conditions or social welfare**.

statementLines: Consider the following information:
I. A series of articles titled “New Lamps for Old” was published in this newspaper between 1893 and 1894 by Aurobindo
Ghose.
II. Gopal Hari Deshmukh, popularly known as Lokhitwadi, played a leading role in the founding of this periodical.
III. The newspaper was from the Bombay Presidency and became an early platform for moderate nationalist political expression.

questions.questionText: Consider the following information: I. A series of articles titled “New Lamps for Old” was published in this newspaper between 1893 and 1894 by Aurobindo Ghose. II. Gopal Hari Deshmukh, popularly known as Lokhitwadi, played a leading role in the founding of this periodical. III. The newspaper was from the Bombay Presidency and became an early platform for moderate nationalist political expression. Which one of the following newspapers is being described by the above given information?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. In the second half of the nineteenth century, newspapers became powerful instruments for spreading political awareness in India. Several early nationalist leaders used the press to question colonial policies and to shape public opinion.**Point I : Aurobindo Ghose**wrote a series of critical articles titled “New Lamps for Old” in**Indu Prakash**during**1893–**1894**. These writings reflected early nationalist critique and intellectual dissatisfaction with existing political approaches.**Point II: Gopal Hari Deshmukh**, widely known by his pen name**Lokhitwadi**, along with other leaders like Vishnu Parshuram Pandit played a key role in founding**Indu Prakash**. He was among the earliest Indian social reformers who believed in using the press as a medium for social and political awakening.**Point III: Indu Prakash**which was an Anglo-Marathi weekly periodical established in the**Bombay Presidency**and functioned as an early voice of**moderate political nationalism**. It provided space for reasoned criticism of British policies rather than revolutionary advocacy.
